data.title: Hormel: Worth Considering At A 4% Yield (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
The Consumer Staples sector has done fine to begin 2025, up 3.6%, but the economically insensitive area has lagged the S&P 500 once again. Despite high-fliers like Walmart (WMT) and Costco (COST), most household-name Staples stocks have merely trodden water over the last handful of months.

Today, I’m revisiting a notable laggard, Hormel Foods (NYSE:HRL). I was bearish on the stock back in November of 2022, highlighting fundamental and technical challenges facing the now $16 billion market cap Packaged Foods and Meats industry firm. I’m upgrading HRL from a sell to a hold after a more than 35% decline over the past 27 months, losing more than 90 percentage points to the S&P 500 over that span.

Headwinds are still in place, such as uncertainty in the pork market, but HRL’s dividend yield is now above 4% and free cash flow appears sufficient to sustain that yield. Still, the valuation is not all that compelling, even with EPS growth expected.

Consumer Staples Lagging in 2025 So Far

Back in December, Hormel reported a weak set of quarterly results. Q3 GAAP EPS came in at $0.40, missing the Wall Street consensus estimate by two pennies while revenue of $3.14 billion, down 2% from the same period a year earlier, was a slight $2.6 million miss. Shares inched higher by 0.5% in the session that followed, and we’ve seen the stock hold a key technical level lately that I will offer color on later in the article.

Looking closer at the quarter, HRL tallied record cash from operations at $1.3 billion and its capex dipped to $256 million. So, free cash flow was helped by those figures; I also like that the company returned $615 million to shareholders.

HRL continues to face challenges such as weak meat markets, particularly in turkey, and the Planters brand has been a drag for several quarters now. For FY 2025, the management team forecasted net sales of $11.9-$12.2 billion with adjusted EPS of $1.58-$1.72. They are hopeful for a positive turnaround in Planters and less volatile pork supply and prices.

On the earnings outlook, analysts are not all that upbeat about Hormel’s prospects, but operating EPS is seen rising 4% this year and accelerating to an 8-10% growth rate from the out year through FY 2027. Profitability trends are decent, with free cash flow being the standout right now.

Nevertheless, the sell side has turned more bearish on the stock over the past 90 days, considering seven EPS downgrades over the past 90 days compared with zero upgrades. But for the first time in the last 10 years, the dividend yield is above 4%.

Hormel: Revenue & EPS Forecasts, Earnings Revision Trends

HRL: Dividend Yield Has Climbed to Above 4%

On valuation, if we assume $1.70 of non-GAAP EPS over the next 12 months and apply a 17 multiple, then shares should trade near $29, close to where HRL is today. I assert that a 17 P/E is right because the EPS growth rate looks to normalize around 9%.

The resulting PEG is a bit below 2, in line with the market average. A single-digit bottom-line growth rate simply does not warrant a mid-20s earnings multiple (the stock’s 5-year average). Shares also trade in line with the sector median price-to-sales ratio.

Hormel: Mixed Valuation Metrics

Key risks for Hormel include uncertainty in the turkey and pork wholesale markets; price volatility is obviously a concern, but supply/demand dynamics may be in flux due to geopolitical risks and animal pandemics. In general, lower cost inflation would be a boon and a healthy global economy would help this geographically diverse business.

Higher market interest rates and ongoing weakness in Planters are problem spots. Hormel plans on increasing advertising investments, so those will have to pan out for operational performance to improve.

Hormel: Competitor Analysis

Looking ahead, corporate event data provided by Wall Street Horizon show a confirmed Q1 2025 earnings date of Thursday, February 27 BMO with a conference call later that morning. No other volatility catalysts are seen on the calendar.

The options market prices in a 5% earnings-related stock price swing when analyzing the at-the-money straddle, expiring soonest after the Q4 report later this month, per data from Option Research & Technology Services (ORATS). Seeking Alpha shows a consensus EPS estimate of $0.39 on revenue of $2.95 billion; those numbers would compare to $0.41 and $3.00 billion a year ago.

Corporate Event Risk Calendar

The Technical Take

With shares being near fair value in my estimation, HRL’s technical view is very weak at first blush. But take a look at where the stock appears to have paused in the broad downtrend – the low from a year ago at $28.51 has held as support for now. The stock is in a precarious position, of course, so there’s a real risk that a bearish breakdown transpires. If so, a measured move downside price target to $20 would be in play based on the $8.50 range/consolidation since Q4 of 2023.

Moreover, the long-term 200-day moving average is negatively sloped, suggesting that the bears control the primary trend and the RSI momentum oscillator at the top of the graph is near oversold conditions after brief spikes that proved to be bear market rallies. While support is near the current price, resistance is way up at $37 and at the downtrend line off the 2022 peak.

HRL: Shares in a Long-Term Downtrend, $28-$29 Support

The Bottom Line

I have a hold rating on Hormel. I see the Consumer Staples stock as fairly priced but with a very high dividend yield that may attract investors if Treasury rates drop. Its chart is bearish, but near-term support is in play.

data.title: Biogen Q4 Earnings Review: Why I'm Calling Time On This 'Powder Puff' Pharma
data.description:
Investment Overview

Biogen (NASDAQ:BIIB) reported its Q4 and full-year 2024 earnings on Wednesday, 12th February. I last covered the company for Seeking Alpha after it had announced its Q3 earnings, which, despite out-performing street estimates on revenues and earnings per share ("EPS"), triggered a market sell-off - from $182 per share, to ~$175 per share, on light full-year guidance.

Despite being forewarned, Wall Street dumped Biogen stock again after Wednesday's Q4 results. Quarterly revenues came in at $2.5bn, up 3% year-on-year, and GAAP EPS came to $1.83, up 7% year-on-year. Non-GAAP EPS was $3.44, up 17%. For the full-year, revenues amounted to $9.7bn, down 2% year-on-year, while GAAP diluted EPS was up 40%, to $11.18, and non-GAAP EPS $16.47, up 12% year-on-year.

In terms of guidance for 2025, Biogen forecast for an EPS of $15.25-$16.25, with total revenues expected to decline by a mid-single digit percentage at constant currency, which management puts down to declining revenues within its Multiple Sclerosis franchise, "partially offset by revenue growth from newly launched products".

Analysis - No Sign Of Meaningful Leqembi Revenues

I have been posting on Biogen for Seeking Alpha since 2021, covering the controversial approval of its Alzheimer's drug, Aduhelm, in June 2021, which sent its stock price soaring from $283 per share, to nearly $400 per share (I held Biogen stock during that period, then sold it).

As we now know, Aduhelm was not only a controversial drug - being potentially unsafe, and barely generating any signs of efficacy in clinical studies, - it was a complete commercial failure, and was removed from the market after making just $300k of sales.

The only other time Biogen stock has spiked was in September 2022, when, alongside Japanese Pharma Eisai, its development partner in the Alzheimer's treatment field, the company shared data from a second AD drug, lecanemab, showing that in its Phase 3 study it had "reduced clinical decline on the global cognitive and functional scale (CDR-SB) by 27% compared to those on placebo at 18 months".

In May 2023, after lecanemab had been granted accelerated approval for commercial use, under the brand name Leqembi, Biogen stock traded at >$315 per share, and the drug received full approval in July 2023. In 2024, however, Biogen reported just $60m of collaboration revenues related to the drug (revenues and costs are split 50/50 between the two companies), despite Eisai's forecast that the drug could achieve $7bn of revenues by 2030.

The reality is that Leqembi has a similar mechanism of action to Aduhelm, targeting amyloid beta ("sticky" clumps of protein, the removal of which has been associated with improvement in a patient's condition) that presents the same safety issues as Aduhelm, related to potentially fatal amyloid related imaging abnormalities ("ARIA") requiring users of the drug to submit to regular brain scans. Furthermore, the drug may be inferior to Eli Lilly's (LLY) Kisunla, approved last year (there are significant doubts around whether Kisunla will drive meaningful revenues, also, given it targets amyloid beta removal).

Since the approval of Leqembi, Biogen's stock has retreated in value to its current value of $138 per share, down >55%.

Multiple Sclerosis, Other Key Drug Franchises In Decline - New Drug Approvals Offer Scant Hope

Biogen's main revenue driver for years has been its multiple sclerosis franchise, but revenues within the franchise have declined from $8.5bn in 2019, to $4.4bn in 2024, although the division still accounted for 45% of all revenues in 2024.

The patent expiries of Tecfidera, Plegridy, and Tysabri have been chiefly responsible for the declines, and although Vumerity, approved in 2019, is viewed as a long-term replacement, it may be the case it never achieves "blockbuster" revenues with rivals such as Swiss Pharma giant Novatis' >$3bn per annum selling Kesimpta, Roche's (OTCQX:RHHBY) Ocrevus, and TG Therapeutics' (TGTX) Briumvi all competing for market share.

Meanwhile within Biogen's Rare Disease portfolio, Spinal Muscular Atrophy therapy Spinraza's revenues have fallen from in every year since 2019, to $1.6bn in 2024, while recently launched Qalsody, indicated for Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis ("ALS"), earned just $32m of revenues, and Skyclarys, a drug indicated for Friedreich's Ataxia ("FA"), acquired as part of Biogen's $7.3bn acquisition of Reata Pharmaceuticals, contributed $383m of revenues, showing no growth between Q3 and Q4.

Within neuromuscular, Zurzuvae, a depression med developed in collaboration with Sage Therapeutics (SAGE), is another crushing disappointment, refused approval in Major Depressive Disorder ("MDD") and eking out just $72m of revenues in 2024. Within the biosimilar division, Rheumatoid Arthritis drug Benepali led the way with $479m of revenues, up 9% year-on-year, but the division as a whole grew just 3% annually and is nowhere being an answer to Biogen's problems.

Elsewhere, Biogen does reasonably well out of its collaboration revenues from Roche in relation to Ocrevus, earning $1.34bn in 2024, up 6%, but Rituxan/Gazyva collaboration revenues fell 4%. Contract manufacturing, royalty and other revenues were down 27%, to $653m.

Pipeline "Promise" Is Thoroughly Underwhelming

Biogen shares the following overview of its pipeline in its Q4 earnings presentation:

There are no product approval highlights to look forward to in 2025, and none in 2026 or 2027, either - it could even be the case Biogen secures no product approvals between now and the end of the decade. It would not surprise me - Biogen is that kind of company.

The areas that Biogen is targeting - systemic lupus erythematosus ("SLE"), IgA nephropathy (IgAN), are tough disease indications to treat, and also subject to intense competition - I would put my faith in the likes of Travere Therapeutics (TVTX) in IgAN, AstraZeneca (AZN), Gilead Sciences (GILD) and several other smaller biotechs in SLE before I would put any faith in Biogen pipeline candidates litifilimab, felzartamab, or Dapirolizumab pegol.

Litfilimab did not garner a mention on Biogen's earnings call, no data was shared in relation to felzartamab, and pegol was described as "only the third agent with a positive global Phase III study in SLE" - not exactly words to set pulses racing on Wall Street.

Sage Acquisition Debacle Sums Up A Company On The Outs

If any further evidence is still required as to why - after three straight posts assigning Biogen a "Buy" rating, I am switching my stance to a "sell", the story of Biogen's attempted buyout of Sage Therapeutics may provide it.

Biogen and Sage have collaborated for years - a collaboration that has been a colossal disappointment for both companies, with only Zurzuvae to show for it, and a host of study failures - Sage stock (which I own a small amount of) is down 90% on a five-year basis.

You might think the companies knew each other well enough and have felt enough egg landing on their faces to be able to hold cordial talks around Biogen potentially acquiring Sage, and putting management out of its misery, but in reality, Sage has brought a lawsuit against Biogen after receiving an "unsolicited" buyout offer.

Biogen's offer was to buyout the shares of Sage it does not already own for a sum of ~$469m, or $7.22 per share - barely a premium to its current traded price, which could be described as an insultingly low offer. A Sage spokesperson has been quoted as saying:

Sage has filed necessary paperwork with the Delaware court seeking a temporary restraining order to preserve our rights and enforce the standstill provision previously agreed to with Biogen

Concluding Thoughts - I Can No Longer Find A Single Reason To Get Excited About Biogen Stock - Sell!

Amidst all of this debacle, Biogen's CEO Christopher A. Viehbacher received $30.5m in pay and stock option awards in his first year on the job in 2022, having replaced Michel Vounatsos, who fell on his sword after the Aduhelm situation got so bad hospitals were refusing to let Biogen sales execs from entering the building.

In fairness, Viehbacher received only $4m in 2023, and the point of this post is not to denigrate Biogen's management or staff, although its hard to avoid the conclusion that Biogen embodies most of what the public - and Wall Street - dislike most about Big Pharma. Bad, overpriced products, endless study failures, lack of innovation, no pipeline promise, billions in wasted R&D, failing share price, no dividend, bad PR.

Biogen reported $2.4bn of cash as of the end of 2024, and only $4.5bn of long-term debt, so, at least the company is not as heavily indebted as other Pharma companies.

Although the company has been struggling, in previous posts I have always arrived at the conclusion that by most investment metrics, its stock looks cheap. Price to sales ratio of just over 2x is far lower than average for a Big Pharma, and forward price to earnings, ~8.7x - also significantly lower than average.

Consider the valuations of other Pharmas driving revenues close to the $10bn mark. Vertex Pharma (VRTX), $11bn revenues in 2024, market cap valuation $118bn. Regeneron (REGN), ~$14bn revenues, $74bn market cap. However, I suspect the reality is that these Pharmas are able to show Wall Street that they mean business, and have long-term plans, products in their pipelines, and astute and caring management that can help establish them amongst the biggest and most relevant Pharma's globally in the long term.

In 2024, Bausch Health (BHC) will likely earn ~$9bn of revenues, yet its market cap valuation is $2.2bn, because it is crippled by debt and Wall Street clearly does not believe there is an easy way out of the hole the company has dug for itself.

Biogen has no such debt concerns, it feels as though the company is simply treading water, with no products or plans to get excited about. Even though this is a company that ought to stand every chance of being successful, after following Biogen for so many years and witnessing almost no positive momentum be established, I think its valuation may well end up in the same place as Bausch Health's - it may be time to break this business up, although it is not obvious what assets would be attractive to any company.

data.title: Microsoft: Owning The AI Stack Helps To Counter The DeepSeek Threat
data.description:
When I last covered Microsoft (NASDAQ:MSFT) in February 2023, I had a hold position, mainly because I had found C3.ai (AI) to be a better investment for exposure to the artificial intelligence theme. I got it wrong; the software giant delivered over 25% gains, mainly because I had failed to anticipate the advantages conferred on its products because of its sizable investment in OpenAI. As a result, it owns the AI stack.

Nearly two years later, Chinese AI startup DeepSeek's LLM (large language model) outperforming OpenAI's equivalent for certain benchmarks has caused Microsoft's stock to suffer more than peer hyperscaler Amazon (AMZN) as charted below. This thesis aims to show that such underperformance may not be justified and that instead, the software giant is better positioned to harvest the benefits of AI diffusing across industries.

I start by highlighting the risks posed to AI infrastructure builders, including Microsoft, because of the distillation effect instilled by DeepSeek.

Understanding the AI Distillation Effect Instilled by DeepSeek as the focus turns to monetization

First, putting things into context, Microsoft suffered as a broader sell-off impacting the Mag7, represented here by the Roundhill Magnificent Seven ETF (MAGS) since around January 28 as charted below. Thus, holding NVIDIA (NVDA) was also impacted as the Chinese company showed that with optimizations at the software algorithm level, namely an improvement in the mixture-of-experts training, it was possible to build AI models with lower-performance GPUs.

To achieve this feat, DeepSeek may have used training data from ChatGPT in a process called distillation whereby knowledge is transferred from larger to smaller models allowing achievement of similar performance but using less computational costs. Now, even if this means that it is not competing on an equal footing with OpenAI (in terms of computing power used), the Chinese company has played an eye-opener role in showing that massive data center build-outs using NVIDIA's cutting-edge GPUs are not prime requisites to train an AI model to achieve top performance.

Thus, it is likely for companies looking to build their AI models to consider options like optimizing their algorithms instead of resorting to sheer brute-force GPU scaling, potentially impacting orders from the semiconductor giant and the related data center supply chain. Other potential losers from DeepSeek’s breakthrough are those actively building data center capacity, including Microsoft, Alphabet (GOOGL) (GOOG), and Amazon at the cost of billions of dollars.

Microsoft's Stock suffered more due to the OpenAI Partnership

To support my point, Microsoft's stock slumping despite beating second-quarter fiscal 2025 earnings by $0.11, hints that investors may be looking beyond purely financial performance, to capital investment made on AI. Thus, after spending around $55.7 billion in FY-24, Microsoft should invest about $80 billion to build AI infrastructures during this fiscal year to train and deploy LLMs and host cloud-based applications globally.

Thus, in the case of distillation becomes widespread, the danger is AI infrastructures being built by Microsoft and other hyperscalers may not attract customers as planned. In this connection, the sell-off of AI-linked stocks suggests the market is recalibrating its expectations to factor in this scenario.

Still, the fact that Microsoft's stock suffered relatively more can be explained by DeepSeek-related cost improvements lowering the barriers to entry for model training, a process that normally consumes significant computational resources. As such, this paves the way for more affordable AI solutions developed by mainstream service providers, instead of most developments being confined to tech giants. Moreover, since DeepSeek is based on open source, it can rapidly become more widely accessible to developers, posing a threat to Microsoft’s exclusive partnership with OpenAI by allowing more organizations to build advanced AI applications without relying on proprietary, and premium-priced services.

Thus, as open-source projects mature, they could grind into Microsoft's competitive moat, forcing it to compete on features like pricing, especially if developers manage to tailor open-source models to cater to their specific needs in a way that exceeds the performance of proprietary solutions.

Microsoft's Advantages of Owning the AI Stack and Having an Integrated Ecosystem

However, firstly, unlike the IT solutions providers, which subscribe to OpenAI's models, Microsoft owns and controls the AI stack given its nearly $14 billion investment in Sam Altman's company. Also, while AI distillation can weaken its model exclusivity, Microsoft has deep integration with OpenAI, giving it first access to GPT-4 Turbo and future model releases. Also, the software giant can boost R&D to ensure that its offerings remain at the cutting edge.

Secondly, Microsoft can counter the open-source threat by focusing on security, compliance, and support, or areas where proprietary products often have an edge. This is exemplified in the server O.S. space, where despite Linux being widely adopted, many large enterprises still rely on Windows-based servers.

Thirdly, the company could come up with alternative pricing strategies including consumption-based charging depending on usage like Salesforce (CRM) has done with Agentforce AI. Furthermore, DeepSeek R1’s availability in the model catalog for Azure AI Foundry alongside 1,800 others sets the stage for potential monetization namely through integrating open-source developments with its proprietary components. To this end, with its ownership and support of the open-source version control software platform GitHub, Microsoft can drive the adoption of its proprietary enhancements.

Fourth, Microsoft’s integrated ecosystem is synonymous with strength.

In this respect, 365 Copilot, which is an artificial intelligence add-on for Microsoft's Office app subscriptions which was launched only in November 2023, could add up to more than $10 billion in annualized revenue by 2026. Now, by being integrated into Microsoft Office applications like Word, Excel, and PowerPoint, 365 Copilot assists users in generating content, analyzing data, and enhancing productivity.

Noteworthily, it is Microsoft's integrated ecosystem with products like Office 365, Azure, and Windows which explains why the company is so profitable.

Product Integration Translates into Better Margins

In this case, once the software is developed, serving an additional customer incurs minimal incremental cost, as it can be distributed over the Internet without the need to allocate labor or physical computational resources for each new subscription as it is all automated. As such, SaaS (Software-as-a-Service) platforms are designed to scale easily, signifying that when the customer base grows, most of the expenses (like software customization and infrastructure) remain relatively fixed, and additional revenue translates directly into profits.

This partly explains why, despite AWS's (Amazon Web Services) much larger cloud computing platform with a 32% market share, it is the software giant that has a higher gross margin as shown below. Detailing further, Microsoft sells the Windows OS, productivity software, and business applications all bundled (or integrated) together, which means it uses cross-selling to generate sales. Thus, by leveraging existing customer relationships, it spends less marketing dollars, and it is more profitable.

Additionally, Microsoft’s net income margin being roughly four times higher than Amazon's also indicates it is far more efficient at converting revenue into profit, highlighting its strong operating model.

To this end, Microsoft’s AI business which mainly includes Copilot and Azure AI services (that help businesses integrate AI into their operations through analytics) has surpassed an annual revenue run rate of $13 billion at the end of December 2024, showing a remarkable growth of 175% YoY as shown below. The fact that operating income grew by 17% YoY while revenue only grew by 12% suggests that Microsoft’s product mix, which now includes a larger dose of AI is shifting toward more profitability.

Cautiously Optimistic Because of Risks

Now, when a company is deriving higher margins from its product suite, it is likely to sustain investment into product innovation, leading to more sustainable growth than Amazon for the AI infrastructure business. This is especially so if a worst-case scenario impacts the demand for GPU-as-a-service because of the distillation effect. Moreover, with a forward P/E around 14% ((31.09-36.14)/36.14) lower than that of Amazon as shown below, investors are currently paying less for each dollar of Microsoft’s future earnings.

This lower valuation, considered in light of its higher profitability, suggests that Microsoft may be undervalued relative to its earnings potential. Thus, as it continually embeds AI across its product line, both growth and profitability prospects should continue improving, thereby narrowing the 14% discount while pushing the stock higher. Thus, based on the discount applied to the share price of $410.5 at the time of writing, I have a target of $468.

Shifting to a cautionary note, from 9x two years back, the forward price-to-sales ratio has jumped to 11x, suggesting a sharp re-rating of the stock based on growth prospects, especially when compared with Amazon's P/S of 3.47x. However, Microsoft's 15% YoY revenue growth far outpacing the IT sector, by nearly 188% justifies its higher P/S. Still, this implies the software stock is likely to exhibit a higher degree of volatility in case of any slowdown in growth due to macroeconomic headwinds. Also, any competitive products likely to reduce its differentiation, for example Google announcing a breakthrough in the way its Gemini AI is integrated with its productivity suite called Workspace could put pressure on the stock, which means continued strong performance is critical.

Along the same lines, Microsoft's guidance for the third quarter fell short of what was expected by analysts on Wall Street, raising doubts about whether the initial momentum noted in AI revenue generation can be sustained, possibly in light of emerging players like DeepSeek. This may occur again as some customers may delay the launch of AI projects while assessing whether they can benefit from distillation.

Still, ending on a note of optimism, with its AI leadership position, Microsoft should continue to benefit as organizations transform their IT into super smart apps to deliver more automation. Also, its strategic partnership with OpenAI, and deep integration of AI across its product suite (Azure cloud services, Office 365 productivity tools, and GitHub developer platforms) positions it to capitalize more effectively on the AI revolution, not only by tapping into new revenue streams but also increasing profits thanks to its interconnected ecosystem which makes it harder for enterprise customers to switch to providers offering disparate solutions.

data.title: Disney: 2025 Deep-Dive Reveals A Wonderful Business And A Real Undervaluation
data.description:
Investment Thesis

The Walt Disney Company (NYSE:DIS) had a great Q1 2025 with the firm increasing profitability tangibly while continuing to grow revenues across their operations.

While the subscriber count fell 1% YoY, Disney appears to be positioning their DTC business to profit from multiple fronts which bolsters my expectations for the remainder of the fiscal year.

I updated my DCF valuation to reflect the improved cash flow generation at the company with the model suggesting a 26% undervaluation may be present in the stock.

In lieu of these fiscal and qualitative business improvements, I upgrade my rating for DIS from a Hold to a Buy.

Business Overview & Economic Moat

Disney is an entertainment icon. Since the firm’s founding back in 1923, the company has developed some of the most iconic franchises and characters while simultaneously monetising these assets incredibly effectively.

Disney’s current operations include their core TV and movie "Entertainment" business, theme-park and resort “Experiences” operations and a range of other multimedia enterprises.

Intellectual Property & Franchises

Moatiness arises for Disney primarily from the value of their powerful intangible assets such as Mickey Mouse, Toy Story, Cars, Winnie the Pooh and more modern additions like the Star Wars and Marvel franchises.

The enduring popularity of established franchises combined with Disney’s ability to create new ones drives their core TV, Movie and attractions businesses. Strong IPs increase the size and reliability of box office returns while providing multiple additional monetisation pathways which Disney can use to increase sales.

The company accordingly has large partnerships with clothing, toy and retail companies such as Hasbro (HAS), Mattel (MAT) and Primark, which allows Disney’s IPs to be translated into products ranging from Mickey Mouse pyjamas to Frozen-themed ski helmets and a staggering volume of toys.

If there is a physical product with space for print or branding, there is most likely a Disney version somewhere in the world.

What Disney has ultimately achieved is a phenomenal integration of their franchises into the everyday lives and psyches of people across the globe. Such a massive, entrenched and beloved presence is practically impossible to replicate and thus earns a wide economic moat.

Parks, Resorts and Cruise Line

Disneyland and Disney World theme parks are one of the best examples of how Disney extracts additional revenues by essentially allowing fans to engage with their beloved TV and movie characters in the physical world.

Disney Cruise Lines is another example of the same phenomenon, where customers can enjoy an all-inclusive cruise experience while in the company of their favorite on-screen heroes and friends.

While competitors certainly exist in the theme park, resort and cruise line industries, I believe these operations enjoy a wide economic moat thanks to their unique focus on bringing the characters and franchises to life. Simply put, a Disney holiday is not necessarily comparable to an otherwise similar offering from a competitor as these rival products are missing one key thing: they have no Disney fanfare in them.

Entertainment Business & DTC Streaming

Circling back to the core TV and Movie business, Disney historically made the majority of their revenues from what is now the declining linear TV business model. Since Netflix (NFLX) pioneered the DTC model, Disney has pivoted to a similar strategy with the launch of Disney+ in 2019.

While profitability is still short of what the lucrative linear model was able to achieve, in less than four years, Disney has produced operating profits from DTC which is impressive.

Disney also has a host of other media operations including Hulu, sports broadcaster ESPN, ABC and a 70% ownership stake in sports streaming company Fubo (which is the result of an agreement to combined Disney’s Hulu + Live TV business with Fubo).

ESPN has historically been a core component of Disney’s now declining linear networks business. Luckily for us, Disney is preparing the launch of an ESPN streaming service in 2025 which promises to combine sports streaming with betting and fantasy interactive elements.

This service will sit alongside Disney+ and be offered as a bundled service for Disney+ and Hulu customers. ESPN+ will also exist alongside Fubo which offers viewers access to the likes of ABC, ESPN, ESPN+, FOX, NBC and CBS without needing a cable TV subscription.

You would be forgiven if you thought I made a typo by including ESPN+ in the list of Fubo content. The Fubo service has indeed been confirmed to also include access to ESPN+.

As a result, I think that the ESPN+ service will be priced much lower (I expect around $12/month) than the approximate $80 per month Fubo charges.

I still think the overall transition to DTC is more the adoption of a new and more convenient format for television, rather than a revolutionary change within the entertainment industry.

Supporting my hypothesis is the integration of advertising into DTC streaming combined with gradual yet continuous price hikes, which ultimately are a necessity to ensure the profitability of these businesses.

With this in mind, I think Disney’s core DTC business finally has a wide economic moat which stems from the value of their IPs as well as a competitive position within the streaming industry. The Fubo deal has fortified this position, while the launch of the new ESPN service should add to this competitive advantage.

Fiscal Analysis – Q4 & FY2024

Disney is a profitable enterprise and, importantly, one that is improving rapidly.

Current gross, operating and net margins are around 36%, 14% and 7% respectively, which, while far cries from pre-Fox acquisition (2019) levels of 45%, 25%, and 22%, are markedly above pandemic-era lows of around 32%, 5% and 0% respectively.

The unfortunate combination that occurred when the additional debt and workload necessary to complete the 21st Century Fox deal combined with the sudden and drastic impact on earnings inflicted by the COVID-19 pandemic left Disney in a compromised state.

However, the recent return to solid growth combined with profitability in the streaming segment has reinvigorated earnings. Current ROA, ROE and ROIC of 3%, 6% and 5% are still depressed and only about half of those returns seen before 2020.

The degradation in ROIC came as a result of the capital acquired during the Fox deal. I expect this to improve towards the 10-14% range as Disney pays off additional debentures, continues to improve net earnings and executes on share buyback initiatives.

In order to buy shares, Disney will need cash, which, luckily the firm is generating at quite some rate. Using figures from the most recent Q1 earnings report, Disney’s FCF/Net income ratio is about 1.5x while their FCF per share is $4.67 compared to an EPS of just $3.07.

This ability to generate free cash flow above their accounted earnings illustrates how profitable the fundamental operations are at The Walt Disney Company. These metrics also compare starkly to rivals Netflix, who only has a FCF/net income ratio of 0.75x.

Seeking Alpha’s Quant shares the sentiment that Disney remains a fundamentally profitable enterprise by providing an “A+” profitability grade at present time. This falls in line with my hypothesis and is a great gut-check to perform at the start of the analysis process.

Analysis of Q1 2025 Earnings

Disney’s latest Q1 earnings report was very positive overall, with the firm exhibiting strong growth across their operational segments while producing a marked improvement in profitability.

Total revenues increased 5% YoY to $24.7 billion, with the results coming thanks to a 9% and 3% increase in entertainment and experiences revenues respectively. These results also came despite the continued impact on earnings produced by the divestiture of the Indian Disney+ Hotstar service.

Strong movie releases in the second half of 2024 including Moana 2, Mufasa: The Lion King and Inside Out 2 continued to buoy entertainment revenues in Q1 while elevated attendance figures at international parks helped boost experiences sales.

Disney’s also produced a massive increase in profitability, with the Entertainment segment in particular seeing a 95% increase in operating income to $1.7 billion.

Such robust results came thanks to the DTC business continuing to improve, with the service generating $293 million in operating income from $6 billion in revenues (segment operating margin of 5%) versus a $138 million loss in the same quarter last year.

Of course, linear networks still contributed a massive $1.1 billion in operating income (operating margin of 50%) despite only accounting for $2 billion in revenues.

Revenue per customer also increased an average of 4% across Disney’s DTC services, which is very positive to see. This decrease came despite a 1% total decrease in Disney+ subscribers.

While the drop in subscriber figures is a shame, strong performance in 2025 both theatrically and thanks to the launch of ESPN+ and partnering with Fubo should help grow Disney’s DTC business substantially.

Even before the launch of their streaming service, ESPN delivered an 8% increase in revenues, which translated to a 15% increase in operating income. Such superb growth came thanks to an increase in advertising revenues and sub-licensing fees, which the segment earns from licensing ESPN content to Disney+ and other providers.

Considering the nuances of the DTC business, I must admit that I really like the direction Disney is taking in monetising this format of media consumption. While the core Disney+ service is integral to the firm, additional licensing of their various content to third-parties and partner firms could become a lucrative source of profitability for the firm.

The experiences segment saw aggregate revenue growth of 3% across domestic and international markets yet generated no operating income growth with their operating margin remaining practically set at 31%.

Higher costs at both their parks, resorts and Cruise Line were unable to be offset by increased attendance volumes and slight ticket price increases.

While Q1 may have been soft for the experiences division, I expect new park expansions set to open in 2025 along with an expanded cruise schedule to help drive growth for the unit through the coming three quarters.

Considering the capital intensive and labor-intensive nature of operating a theme park and cruise line, it isn’t inconceivable to hypothesize that their operating economics are unlikely to improve in 2025 as a result of sticky supply side inflation.

Overall, I found Disney’s Q1 report to be largely positive with perhaps the only slight blemish being the decrease in Disney+ subscribers, at least at face value. The Fubo partnership combined with the launch of ESPN+ and what seems to be a blockbuster release schedule for 2025 should boost revenues, the subscriber count and overall profitability in the coming quarters.

Balance Sheet & 2025 Outlook

Disney’s balance sheet also remains healthy, with the firm continuing to reduce their leverage ratio from highs of around 2.4x in 2020 closer to their long-term target of around 1.6x.

I also want to reiterate that the firm’s debt to equity ratio remains a wonderful 0.4x, while interest coverage is about 5x considering their TTM earnings.

Q1 saw Disney completed $794 million worth of share buybacks, which was falls in line with their goal of delivering $3 billion in buybacks in 2025.

Earnings are expected to increase about 9% in FY25, which, I think, is achievable given the firm’s focus on continued DTC profitability. While I expect operating income in the experiences division to increase, I don’t see an expansion of margins in this segment as likely.

Valuation

In order to start my examination into what value may lie in DIS shares, I went to Seeking Alpha’s Valuation tab for the company’s stock with the Quant currently providing a “D” valuation.

The pessimistic letter grade appears to be coming from comparison of DIS against the entire communication services sector, as the 19.9x P/E non-GAAP TTM seems reasonable to me and is down 34% from Disney’s 5Y mean.

The P/CF TTM of 13x is also fair considering the rate at which Disney is improving profitability and cash flows.

Fundamentally, these ratios appear fair for a business estimated to grow at a rate of about 9% over the next five years, versus the long-term average S&P 500 growth rate of 8% and the accompanying average 17x P/E ratio.

Seeking Alpha’s Advanced Chart function illustrates the relative TTM underperformance of the company’s shares when compared to the wider market, as represented by the S&P 500 (SPY).

DIS stock has seen some volatility due to the slightly mixed quarterly results produced in 2024 with the latter half of the year seeing strong performance by the stock in response to more positive earnings reports.

DCF Model

I also completed a DCF model for DIS where I found an intrinsic value estimate for the company’s shares relative to the firm’s total free cash flows from today till judgment day discounted to present value.

FCF data and balance sheet items came from Seeking Alpha’s Financials tab for DIS stock, with the resulting average growth rate of 8.36% being largely in line with my long-term expectations for FCF growth.

As a result, I used an 8% projected future growth rate, a 3% terminal growth rate (my expectations for inflation in the U.S. over the next 50 years) and a discount rate of 7.98%. This percentage is the average-annualised return produced by the S&P 500 over the last 93 years and thus represents the opportunity cost against which I compare other more risk-prone single equity investments.

My IV estimate came out to $148 per share, which, I think, is fair given my conservative inputs.

While short-term (1-12 month) returns by DIS stock will be sensitive to macroeconomic developments and any lackluster performance of new theatrical releases, I think the long-term (1-10 years) looks much more positive.

A renewed focus on universally appealing content combined with improving enterprise economics suggests that the company is making decisions at the present time with an eye to genuinely good long-term business performance.

Disney Risk Profile

I see the firm facing three key threats: market cyclicality, competitive pressures and the risk of failed execution.

Market Cyclicality

While movie releases and television viewership tends to be less dramatically affected by cyclical consumer spending habits, the Experiences division in particular can suffer from relatively large swings in profitability.

The continuous appreciation in the price of park tickets, resorts stays and cruise line trips further compounds this issue as such large spending is often the first to be eliminated by income restricted consumers.

Such a scenario would most certainly impact earnings tangibly (as seen in 2020 and 2021) although I would expect the firm to react by reducing ticket prices in a bid to maintain revenues while taking a temporary hit on margins.

Competitive Forces

Competition is also rife within the entertainment industry with established players such as Netflix, Warner Bros. Discovery (WBD) and Paramount (PARA) all vying for a larger share of the streaming market share.

While Amazon's (AMZN) Prime Video and Netflix lead the industry, Disney has a robust second position with a serious view to increase their market share in the future thanks to strategic partnerships and strong IPs. The firm is also great at generating cash from operations, which is something rivals such as Netflix and Warner Brothers have struggled to do as effectively.

Execution Risk

Finally, Disney faces perhaps the greatest risk of all from themselves in the form of failed execution leading to subpar box office results and weaker earnings. Continued missteps with theatrical releases (as witnessed with more left-leaning content produced between 2015-2021) has the potential to create real reputation damage to their hugely valuable IPs, which in turn would undermine their entire economic moat and business strategy.

Considering all these threats, I feel the need to provide a high-risk rating to The Walt Disney Company and, as always, urge my readers to conduct their own research into these topics.

Summary

Disney produced solid earnings in Q1 while providing a genuine explanation of how they will continue growing in 2025. Improved economic performance was complemented by qualitative wins such as the Fubo deal, the upcoming launch of ESPN+ and the robust theatrical release schedule, which should all cumulatively help buoy the company through the fiscal year.

My updated DCF IV estimate of $148 per share suggests there is a 26% undervaluation at present time, which, combined with the positive outlook for 2025 and beyond suggests there may very well be a GARP opportunity in DIS stock at present time.

With this in mind, I feel it is appropriate to upgrade my rating for The Walt Disney company from a Hold to a Buy.

data.title: Herc Holdings Looks Set To Continue Adding Value This Year
data.description:
Back in June of last year, one company that I decided to reaffirm as a ‘buy’ candidate was Herc Holdings (HRI). This is a business that owns and rents out equipment, which to me is a fascinating space. After all, in many cases, it is impractical or financially impossible to buy equipment if you're not going to be using it on a regular basis. So instead, Herc Holdings is there to fill that need. In that article, I was impressed by the strong financial performance that the company was exhibiting. Furthermore, I believed that shares were attractively priced. Since then, that call has played well. While the S&P 500 is up 11.2%, shares of Herc Holdings are up a whopping 54.5%.

Of course, this doesn't mean that the ride has been without incident. On February 13th, for instance, shares of the business promptly fell 6% after management announced financial results for the final quarter of the company's 2024 fiscal year. Even though the company exceeded analysts’ expectations when it came to revenue, they fell short when it came to earnings and adjusted earnings. However, on February 14th, the stock did post a partial recovery, likely because the market still recognizes that this is an undervalued play. At the end of the day, I do think that the downturn was an overreaction and I would argue that the stock deserves enough upside to justify keeping the ‘buy’ rating I had on it previously.

A mixed quarter

The final quarter of the 2024 fiscal year was rather mixed for equipment rental company Herc Holdings. Take revenue as an example. During the quarter, the business booked sales of $951 million. In addition to representing a 14.4% increase over the $831 million reported the previous year, the amount of revenue reported by the company came in $26.1 million greater than what analysts were hoping to see. This year-over-year improvement in revenue was largely the result of the firm's equipment rental activities, with revenue associated with those climbing 12.2% from $748 million to $839 million. Management claimed that this was largely the result of increased volume that contributed 11.6% to the firm's top line. Higher pricing also aided to the tune of 2.1%.

It is important to note here that revenue can be rather volatile in this space. This is because, as it becomes clear that certain equipment is no longer optimal to have, firms in this industry do tend to sell them off. But because it is a regular revenue stream as opposed to one off asset sales, it is counted in revenue. Year over year, sales of rental equipment for the company jumped 41.2% from $68 million to $96 million. Sales of new equipment, parts, and supplies, remained flat at $9 million year over year. And service and other revenue inched up only slightly from $6 million to $7 million. It is likely because of the volatility that comes from the sale of rental equipment that the market didn't really put a lot of weight on the stronger than expected revenue for the quarter. Instead, the market's interest was in the bottom line for the company.

During the quarter, Herc Holdings generated a loss per share of $1.62. That's far worse than the $3.20 per share reported the previous year, and it translates to a miss compared to what analysts were expecting of $5.72. While the company did see some core costs increase marginally, the biggest contributor to this weakening was a loss on assets held for sale amounting to $194 million. This was a loss that the company booked in order to adjust down the estimated fair value of its Cinelease Studio Entertainment business. This is a studio management and lighting and grip business used for the entertainment industry.

Management is actively exploring the sale of these operations and hopes to complete this process sometime this year. This was something the company announced that it was working on in the final quarter of 2023. But the timing of this impairment and the fact that the sales process has taken so long indicates to me that the firm is having a difficult time finding a buyer at a decent price. This is disappointing. However, it is worth noting that it is a small piece of the overall pie. According to management, this operation accounts for only about 4% of the company's original equipment costs.

On an adjusted basis, the picture fell short as well. Adjusted earnings per share increased from $3.24 per share to $3.58 per share. Even though this represents an increase, it still fell short of analysts’ expectations by $0.36 per share. These profitability figures mean that net profits went from $91 million to negative $46 million, while adjusted net profits increased only a bit from $92 million to $102 million. Other profitability metrics were, fortunately, quite positive. Operating cash flow, for instance, rose from $290 million to $331 million. If we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get an increase from $334 million to $363 million. And finally, EBITDA for the business grew from $382 million to $438 million.

In the chart above, you can see financial results for 2024 compared to 2023. With the exception of net profits, the company saw year over year improvements across the board. The overall growth of the business was driven mostly by higher volumes of equipment rental amounting to 9.3%. However, higher pricing aided to the tune of 3.2% as well. This, to me, suggests that the overall fundamental health of the company is quite positive. Moving forward, we shouldn't expect anything less.

Management specifically pointed out growth opportunities across multiple industries as catalysts for further upside. They said that a pipeline of new construction and maintenance projects offer attractive growth opportunities. Investments in chip plants, data centers, renewable energy, electric vehicles and battery technology, utilities, health care, general infrastructure, and even LNG projects, should bode well for shareholders. Because of this, management expects to spend between $700 million and $900 million this year on capital expenditures. Net of asset sales, this number should be between $400 million and $600 million. This does not factor in the prospect for the acquisition of other businesses. During 2024, Herc Holdings allocated $600 million to buy up nine other firms. And over the last three years, it has allocated a whopping $1.55 billion toward acquisitions.

If everything goes according to plan, equipment revenue this year will be between 4% and 6% higher than it was in 2024. This ignores the Cinelease Studio Equipment business that management is still trying to sell. Also ignoring that, management believes that EBITDA will be between $1.575 billion and $1.65 billion. If we take the midpoint of that and assume that other profitability metrics will climb at the same rate, we would get adjusted net profits of $373.8 million and adjusted operating cash flow of $1.31 billion.

In the chart above, you can see what this means for the valuation of the company. I also included valuation figures for 2023 and 2024 for context. Even though the stock might not be the cheapest relative to adjusted earnings, it is very cheap relative to cash flows. But that's not all. In the table below, I compared Herc Holdings to three similar businesses. What I found was that, on both a price to earnings basis and on a price to operating cash flow basis, only one of the three businesses was cheaper than it is. And when it came to the EV to EBITDA approach, I found that Herc Holdings ended up being the cheapest of the group.

Company Price / Earnings Price / Operating Cash Flow EV / EBITDA
Herc Holdings 15.5 4.4 6.2
Hertz Global Holdings (HTZ) 9.4 0.5 8.6
United Rentals (URI) 19.1 10.9 8.7
H&E Equipment Services (HEES) 22.2 6.4 6.9

Takeaway

All things considered, I can understand why the market may have been a little frustrated with the financial results reported for the final quarter of last year. But the big picture for Herc Holdings seems to me to be quite healthy. The company continues to grow nicely and shares are cheap. Add on top of these key growth catalysts as I outlined already, and I do think that the picture is attractive enough to justify keeping the firm rated a ‘buy’ for now.

data.title: Schrödinger: An Interesting Investment With Immense Potential
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Upon the analysis presented in this article, I rate Schrödinger Inc. (NASDAQ:SDGR) with a Hold rating. The company has three experimental drugs in Phase 1 of clinical trials. This year, the company will report the results, concerning Phase 1 of clinical trials in its three experimental drugs. I believe it is convenient to wait until the company reports its drug trial findings.

Due to the significant expenses that SDGR has incurred to conduct its drug trials, the company will likely continue to report losses for several years. SDGR's success will depend on the results of the clinical trials. As such, investors need to understand that investing in SDGR incurs high-risk levels. A scientific study reports that clinical trials that use small molecules for oncology drugs have 14.3% of receiving approval by the FDA ((across all three phases)). Therefore, the probability that SDGR conducts the three phases of clinical drugs successfully in at least one of its three experimental drugs is 37.07%. Based on this consideration, I think there is not a clear signal to indicate whether SDGR is an excellent investment or not.

Understanding the Business

SDGR is an American company headquartered in New York, U.S. The company has two business segments: software and drug discovery. In 2023, the software segment accounted for 73% of the revenues, and drug discovery represented 27%, as shown in the following chart.

The software segment provides services, for the most part, to biopharmaceutical companies. It focuses on developing software in all stages of molecule discovery. In addition, SDGR develops programs for industrial purposes, which generates revenues to a lesser extent.

In the software business, the company uses two key methods: physics-based methods and machine learning. SDGR developed its platform using physics-based computational methods to predict the binding affinity of a drug molecule, and according to the company, its platform has a high degree of accuracy. The binding affinity is extremely important for developing medical drug candidates. However, this software has computational limitations; it cannot analyze a large number of molecules. Due to this reason, SDGR relies on machine-learning algorithms to filter and find attractive molecules that can be used in a subsequent step in its physics-based method to predict the binding affinity of a drug molecule.

In the 2023 Annual Report, SDGR indicated that the top 20 biopharmaceutical companies measured by revenues used its software products and services.

The second segment, drug discovery, has two sub-segments: drug discovery collaborations, and a proprietary drug development program. The collaboration program consists of partnerships between SDGR and other firms. This program provides SDGR's customers with the following services:

  • Access to the software platform
  • Access to computer power
  • Early access to cutting technology
  • Drug designs for particular protein targets.

The partnership consists, for the most part, of drug candidate developments by SDGR on behalf of its customers. For this goal, SDGR relies on its platform, computer power, and its extensive experience in the field.

SDGR does not receive a fixed payment for these collaborations. In the 2023 Annual Report, SDGR reveals that it has received equity stakes from other companies as payment for its collaborations. As of December 31, 2023, SDGR had equity stakes in the following companies:

Equity stakes are not the only source of revenue that SDGR receives in these agreements. In addition, SDGR obtains revenues by providing the following services: research, discovery, development, commercial milestones, royalties, etc. For instance, in September 2022, SDGR agreed to collaborate with Lilly (LLY). For this collaboration, SDGR will be responsible for the drug discovery phase, while Lilly will be responsible for the other phases of the drug development. SDGR received an upfront payment for this collaboration and could receive up to $425.0 million and royalties if Lilly develops the drug successfully.

In most cases, the upfront payment is a small amount. On the other hand, the larger payment comes when the collaborator receives the drug approval. However, it requires years to develop a new medical drug successfully. As such, SDGR's collaborations require years to bring fruit.

Finally, SDGR has its proprietary drug program. The company is developing its own medical drugs. It has three drugs in Phase 1 of clinical trials.

Financial Overview

From a financial perspective, SDGR has reported losses in five of the last six years, as shown in the first chart below. The company had positive net income only in 2023, and it was due to gains in its securities investment. The operating expenses are the reasons for such results. Between 2019 and 2024Q3 TTM, the operating expenses have grown by 291%, while the revenues increased by 126%, as shown in the second chart below. To grant context, in 2020, the operating expenses were 1.15 times the revenues, and in 2024Q3 TTM, it grew to 1.78 times.

SDGR saw 268% and 417% growths on its general and administrative expenses, and research and development expenses, respectively, between 2019 and 2024Q3. To contextualize the previous figures, in 2024Q3 TTM, the company reported revenues of $193.35 million, while its research and development expenses were $203.91 million.

The company has invested enormous resources to finance its proprietary drug discovery program. For instance, in the first nine months of 2024, SDGR's research and development expenses were $152.42 million versus $119,22 million in revenues. The research and development expenses correspond, for the most part, to finance SDGR's proprietary drug discovery program.

Drug Development

It takes 10-15 years, on average, to develop a medical drug ((the three phases of clinical trials)). It is a long process. One study estimates that it takes, on average, 9.5 years for innovative drugs. I bring these numbers to indicate that if SDGR's drug discovery segment is successful, SDGR will have to wait several years to receive most of its revenues.

As indicated in the previous section, SDGR has burned financial resources since 2019. The company has invested substantial resources in its drug discovery segment with the hope of seeing economic resources in the future. From a financial point of view, the 2024Q3 Annual Report of SDGR reveals the following:

As of September 30, 2024, we had cash, cash equivalents, restricted cash, and marketable securities of $398.4 million. We believe that our existing cash, cash equivalents, and marketable securities as of September 30, 2024 will be sufficient to fund our operating expenses and capital expenditure requirements through at least the next 24 months. However, we have based this estimate on assumptions that may prove to be wrong, and our operating plans may change as a result of many factors currently unknown to us. As a result, we could deplete our capital resources sooner than we currently expect.

According to the previous quote, SDGR would have financial resources for at least up to September 2026. The following chart shows how SDGR saw a decline in its cash and cash equivalents, marketable securities, and restricted cash. These are the financial resources that the company has used to cover its losses. In 2020, these components were $643.19 million, and it declined to $398.44 million in 2024Q3, which is a drop of $244.75 million. Based on a visual analysis of the chart, I think that SDGR will have financial resources for at least another 4-6 years.

Proprietary Development Program

In the drug discovery segment, SDGR is developing its own medical drugs. For this initiative, SDGR has dedicated significant financial resources. The company has three drug candidates in phase 1, namely: SGR-1505 (MALT1), SGR-2921 (CDC7), and SGR-3515 ((WEE1/MYT1)), as shown in the next table.

This year will be important for SDGR's drug development program. The company will release the clinical results for its three programs that are in phase 1. The probabilities can shed light on whether the company can successfully develop at least one of its drugs that are in the three phases of clinical trials. This result will determine whether the drug receives FDA approval. In 2011, a scientific study reported that clinical trials that use small molecules for oncology drugs have a 14.3% approval rate. SGR-1505 (MALT1), SGR-2921 (CDC7), and SGR-3515 ((WEE1/MYT1)) were built using small molecules and are oncology drugs. When using the approval rate, the probability that DSGR will successfully develop at least one drug of its three drugs that are in the first phase to receive FDA approval is:

  • 1-((1-0.143))* ((1-0.143))* ((1-0.143)) = 1- 0.6294 =0.3707 or 37.07%

The previous estimation suggests that SDGR will have a 37.07% probability of success. However, in my opinion, some factors incline me to think that SDGR should have a higher success rate. The scientific paper was published in 2011. In that year, the world did not have the computer power that it has today. In addition, SDGR has been enhancing its software and has accumulated years of experience. I believe these factors contribute to increasing the probability of success.

Considering the arguments exposed in this section, in my opinion, it is too early to be optimistic. My estimations suggest that the company has a probability of success of 37.07% to receive FDA approval for at least one drug. In my opinion, this probability is still low, as it does not even reach 50%. As indicated in the previous paragraph, some factors suggest that the probability is higher. Nonetheless, I think that it is necessary to be as conservative as possible.

Drug Collaborations

In the 2023 Annual Report, SDGR indicated that it has 19 active collaborations to develop medical drugs. From these programs, the company reported that it is eligible to receive future royalties on commercial sales in 12 programs. The most recent agreements include alliances with Bristol-Myers Squibb Company (BMY), Lilly, and Novartis (NVS).

With BMY, SDGR can receive up to $482 million in milestone payments for one neurology drug target. In 2022, Lilly signed an agreement with SDGR, in which SDGR will optimize small molecule compounds for an immunology target. Lilly will implement the preclinical, clinical, and commercialization phases. If Lilly has success and receives approval from the FDA, SDGR could receive up to $420 million.

In 2024, SDGR agreed with Novartis to develop small molecule compounds for targets in diverse therapeutic areas. SDGR will receive $150 million in upfront payment from Novartis. In addition, SDGR could receive up to $2.27 billion in milestone payments.

Software

The software segment of SDGR has had excellent results, as shown in the next chart. Between 2019 and 2024Q3 TTM, the revenues of this segment grew at a 21.66% compound annual growth rate (CAGR). In my opinion, this result reveals an astonishing growth rate. In addition, in the software segment, the cost of revenues is low compared to its revenues. In 2024Q3 TTM, the ratio cost of revenues-to-revenues was 19.07% in this segment.

Risk

SDGR has several risks that can undermine its business operations. In my opinion, the company faces two main risks: the cancellation of drug collaborations and SDGR's financial resources.

The medical drug collaborations that SDGR has with several companies can be canceled at any moment. For instance, in 2020, SDGR established a collaboration program with BMY. The agreement stipulated that SDGR had to research and develop small molecule compounds for biological targets in different areas. However, BMY canceled all the targets, except one medical drug target. Due to this cancellation, SDGR received only small upfront payments of what it would have received if BMY had developed the medical drugs successfully.

As indicated in the Financial Overview section, SDGR has reported net losses for several years. These losses, for the most part, have reduced SDGR's current assets by 33.18% between 2020 and 2024Q3. However, the company has dedicated significant financial results to its drug discovery program. Until the company does not have success in its drug proprietary program, or its drug collaborative program, SDGR will continue to report financial losses. If this scenario continues for several years, the company could run out of money and file for bankruptcy.

Peer Analysis

This section will conduct the peer analysis among Schrödinger Inc., Cadence Design Systems (CDNS), Simulations Plus (SLP), AbbVie (ABBV), Ono Pharmaceutical (OTCPK:OPHLF), Exelixis (EXEL), and Repare Therapeutics (RPTX). Schrödinger Inc. mentions CDNS and SLP as direct competitors in its software business. In addition, Schrödinger Inc. mentions ABBV, OPHLF, EXEL, and RPTX as competitors in its proprietary drug development program. The comparisons are documented in the table below.

SDGR is not a trading company with analysts writing frequently. On the other hand, the 1-year and 5-year stock returns exhibit negative values, indicating the SDGR has had a poor performance.

The valuation metrics show mixed results. SDGR has a 3.97 price-to-book ratio, the fourth-largest value among its peers ((in the middle)). Meanwhile, the EV/Sales ratio suggests that SDGR is overvalued. On the other hand, the company reports excellent growth revenue metrics among its peers. The company reported the highest 5-year revenue growth.

SDGR has the third-lowest gross profit margin, with 64.91%. Albeit, in my opinion, its gross profit margin is high. Due to the high operating expenses, SDGR has a negative return on equity (ROE). The firm has the second-lowest ROE and the second-lowest return on total capital.

From a debt perspective, SDGR bodes well. It has a 4.11 current ratio, indicating that it has substantial current assets, and can pay its short-term debts with minor problems. The company has the third-largest current ratio among its peers.

Overall, the company performs well when compared to its peers. It exhibits a 20.16% 5-year revenue growth. In addition, SDGR has significant current assets to pay its short-term liabilities. However, SDGR's success depends, for the most part, on its drug discovery segment.

Conclusion

In light of the analysis presented in this article, I conclude that it is too early to consider SDGR to be an excellent investment opportunity. On the one hand, the software products and services segment has had excellent financial results, growing its revenues at a 21.66% CAGR. On the other hand, the drug discovery segment is still in its infancy. SDGR's drug development program has three experimental drugs in phase 1 of clinical trials. I believe it is necessary to wait until the company shows the results of phase 1 to reevaluate SDGR. The probability that the FDA approves at least one of the three experimental drugs that SDGR has in phase 1 of clinical trials is 37.07%, which is below 50%.

Despite the fact that the software products and services segment of SDGR is profitable, the drug discovery segments require significant financial resources. Due to this reason, the company has reported financial losses for several years. If this situation continues in the future, SDGR can end in bankruptcy. However, as indicated in the previous paragraph, I consider that it is necessary to wait and see the results of the clinical trials. Based on this consideration, I rate SDGR with a hold rating.

data.title: MongoDB: Sell Before The Weak Fundamentals Catch Up With The Stock (Technical Analysis)
data.description:
Thesis

MongoDB, Inc. (NASDAQ:MDB) stock has gained around 20% in the past six months, but is still down more than 35% for the past year as investors look for a rebound. In the below analysis, I determine that the current technicals are strong in their outlook for the stock. However, as I analyze the fundamentals, I find that revenue growth is decelerating and their guidance for FY2025 Q4 implies that it will further decelerate in the coming quarter. Guidance also shows that adjusted EPS growth is expected to fall into negative territory. For the valuation, while the P/S ratio is at low levels relative to the past three years, it still fails to reflect the upcoming slowdown in growth and is at a major premium to peers. Therefore, I believe it would be wise to take advantage of the technical strength to sell MDB stock before the reality of the deteriorating growth sets in.

Technicals

Charting

Trends

MDB stock broke out of its longer-term uptrend back in mid-December. That uptrend dated back to late August, but this trend line has now become resistance after the breakdown. The stock has, however, developed a new nearer term uptrend since early January and since its slope is steeper than the former uptrend line, the stock has a chance of reentering the longer uptrend at some point.

Support

In addition to the nearer term uptrend line, there are two other significant support levels. The first is in the mid-250s and represents major support in early October and resistance in early January. The other support area is in the low 230s. This price level acted as key support in late August as well as at the end of December.

Resistance

Other than the former uptrend line, there are two more noteworthy resistance levels. The nearer one is in the high 290s, very near the current stock price, and was resistance from September to mid-November. The other resistance area is at 350 and represents a major downside gap. This level was also resistance in late November and since the volume was overwhelming on the day of the gap, its importance is high.

Takeaway

I would say that this chart is a net positive one for MDB. The stock has developed a new uptrend and has hopes of reclaiming the longer-term uptrend line. However, there is quite a balance of support and resistance levels, and so the stock's movement maybe slightly limited.

Moving Averages

Simple Moving Average (SMA)

The 50-day SMA has remained below the 200-day SMA for the past six months, showing sustained bearishness for the longer term. However, the gap between the SMAs decreased significantly from August to December and is currently holding stable, showing that bearish momentum has receded and has been highly contained.

Exponential Moving Average (EMA)

The 9 day EMA had a bullish crossover with the 21 day EMA in late January, an indication of near term strength. The gap between the EMAs has expanded at a slow rate, reflecting a gradual increase in near term bullish momentum. The stock is now trading above both the EMAs and SMAs and so the stock is in a solid technical position currently.

Bollinger Bands

The stock broke back above the midline in mid-January, another signal of strength. This signal is also a confirmation of the nearer term uptrend, and the midline should hold as support moving forward if the uptrend is a strong one. The stock is currently relatively near the upper band but is not there yet, showing that it's not overbought despite the recent rally.

Takeaway

I believe these moving averages are net positive in their signalling. The SMAs show that longer term bearish momentum has receded significantly, the EMAs show accelerating near term bullishness, and the Bollinger Bands provided confirmation of the start of the nearer term uptrend in the stock.

Indicators

Moving Average Convergence Divergence (MACD)

The MACD has a bullish crossover with the signal line in early January. After the crossover, the gap between the lines expanded quite a bit as shown by the histogram but in the past few weeks, the gap has had some periods of expansion and contraction. The histogram has been mixed as of late showing that bullishness has been a bit unstable.

Relative Strength Index (RSI)

The RSI currently is at 66.15 after it reclaimed the 50 level in mid-January as a sign that the bulls have reclaimed control from the bears. The RSI has been on an uptrend of its own since the late December low in the indicator, and so strength may continue to build for the stock moving forward.

Stochastics

Overall, the stochastics is in a bullish position currently. At the start of the new year, there was a major bullish crossover as the %K crossed above the %D. Since then, there have been bearish crossovers that turned out to be mainly false signals that reflect only a pause in bullish momentum rather than a reversal. There has been an abundance of crossovers lately, but currently, since lines are within the 80+ zone, the stochastics reflects that the bulls are in firm control of the stock.

Takeaway

As a whole, these indicators were fairly bullish in their outlook for the stock. The MACD is still in a bullish state overall, the RSI is in a promising strengthening trend, and the stochastics is in the 80+ zone. Therefore, further technical strength is likely at this point.

Fundamentals

Earnings

Quarterly Results

MDB is expected to release their FY2025 Q4 results on March 5th. They released their Q3 earnings back in December and showed mixed results overall. They reported total revenues of $529.4 million, up 22% YoY and a net loss per share of $0.13 versus a $0.41 loss in the year ago period. As shown in the above chart, revenue growth is at resilient levels but overall, despite some improvement over the past quarter, it is at below average levels and is still in a worrying downtrend. For EPS, Q3 represented the narrowest net loss per share in the past three years and showed great improvement both YoY and QoQ, and so there are promising signs. EPS also seems to be in an uptrend and so the company has the potential of breaking even, moving forward. In non-GAAP terms, EPS increased from $0.96 to $1.16 YoY, reflecting a 20.8% growth rate. All of these results beat expectations with revenue beating by $33.65 million, GAAP EPS beating by $0.72 and adjusted EPS beating by $0.47, showing that performance versus consensus estimates was strong.

Guidance

MDB provided guidance for their FY2025 Q4. They expect revenues to be between $515 million and $519 million. In their FY2024 Q4, they reported revenues of $458 million, and so this guidance implies a revenue growth rate of between 12.4% and 13.3%. This is a major slowdown from Q3 and shows that the downtrend continues in revenue growth, a worrying sign. They also project that non-GAAP EPS will be between $0.62 and $0.65 for their Q4 and since they had a non-GAAP EPS of $0.86 in FY2024 Q4, this guidance implies adjusted EPS growth of between -24.4% and -27.9%. This is a major difference from Q3's YoY growth of above 20% and shows that the outlook is very concerning for MDB. Overall, I believe both the revenue and EPS guidance are worrying as they imply slowing growth and a plunge into negative growth, respectively.

Valuation

Price To Sales Ratio (P/S)

The P/S ratio is currently at very low levels in relation to the past three years of data. It has a reading of 11.21 as of now and has climbed somewhat to start the new year. Despite this relatively low level in the multiple, I still believe MDB stock is overvalued. As discussed above, revenue growth is currently in a downtrend and quite near three-year lows. Furthermore, the revenue guidance that they provided implies a major growth slowdown coming in their Q4. The implied growth of between 12.4% and 13.3% is much below Q3's growth rate of 22%. It would also likely be the worst revenue growth recorded by MDB in the past three years. Therefore, while the P/S ratio is at low levels, I believe it's not low enough as it doesn't reflect this slowdown sufficiently. This disconnect leads me to believe that the stock is overvalued relative to growth. Currently, the Information Technology sector's median P/S ratio is only 3.36 and so MDB is trading at an over 200% premium to peers. Given the deteriorating growth outlook for the company, this premium is hard to justify currently. Therefore, I conclude that MDB is overvalued at current levels, as the P/S ratio is at risk of contraction as the reality of the fundamentals sink in.

Conclusion

In the above analysis, I have determined that the technicals are strong for the stock. Instead of believing that investors should buy into this strength, I believe investors should use this as a selling opportunity. As discussed above, the fundamentals of the business are becoming concerning as revenue growth deceleration is expected to continue and adjusted EPS growth is projected to drop into deeply negative territory. While the P/S ratio is at relatively low levels compared to its history, I believe it still doesn't adequately show the concerning direction for the company. Therefore, I believe a sell rating is warranted, as investors should use the strong technicals to sell MDB as the fundamentals deteriorate.

data.title: Federal Reserve Watch: Trump Starts To Move
data.description:
The Federal Reserve has been dealing with the fact that the current monetary situation began with a period in which the Fed pumped lots and lots of money into the banking system, producing a very rapid growth in the money stock, followed by a period of almost three years of quantitative tightening which has resulted in a very modest rise in the money stock.

Overall, during the past five years, the M2 stock of money has risen at a compound rate of growth of 7.0 percent from December 30, 2019 to December 23, 2024.

Here is what the chart looks like.

So, over the past five years, the Federal Reserve has put plenty of money into the economic system.

And, this is why the Federal Reserve is worried about the United States becoming more inflated.

Since March 2022, the Federal Reserve has been conducting a monetary policy of quantitative tightening.

That is, since the middle of March 2022, the Federal Reserve has been reducing the size of the Fed's securities portfolio, reducing the amount of excess reserves in the commercial banking system.

Here is a picture of this activity.

In pursuing its monetary "tightening" in this way, the Federal Reserve has been able to reduce the amount of excess reserves in the commercial banking system so as to reduce the threat of future inflation, while at the same time allowing the economy to continue to grow in the range of 2.00%-2.50%.

This program has resulted in a falling rate of inflation while at the same time keeping the unemployment rate near a 50-year low.

Overall, the result of this approach has kept the economy in a pretty good position.

But, there still remain lots and lots of excess reserves in the banking system.
