In other words, the Federal Reserve still has a long way to go to get the banking system back to a more stable position, one that does not contain all the excess money which could cause further serious rounds of inflation in the American economy.

The Federal Reserve has slowed down the rate at which it is reducing the size of the securities portfolio. This adjustment came about in the middle of September.

Note that this adjustment to the pace of the reduction in the securities portfolio appears to have come as Federal Reserve officials moved to reduce the level of its policy rate of interest...the Federal Funds rate.

Here is the picture of the upper bound of the policy rate of interest.

So, since the middle of September 2024, the Federal Reserve has reduced the level of its policy rate of interest three times, the last move coming in December 2024.

Recently, the Fed passed on lowering its policy rate of interest another notch as it did not believe that further movements were justified at this time given the current state of the economy and the monetary system.

Analysts seem to believe that maybe the Federal Reserve has reached the lowest level it is going to reach this year for the policy rate.

As I have been writing, inflationary expectations have seemed to pick up in the financial markets, working against any "new" changes in the level of the interest rates.

With these inflationary expectations apparently moving to become even higher, the justification of further decreases in the policy rate of interest seem very doubtful at this time.

However, there remains the need to continue to reduce the size of the Fed's securities portfolio.

On March 16, 2022, the size of the Fed's securities portfolio was right around $8.5 trillion. This is up from a size of $3.8 trillion on March 20, 2019.

Now, on February 12, 2025, the size of the securities portfolio was $6.5 trillion.

So, the Fed's buildup added $4.7 trillion to the securities portfolio.

And, due to the quantitative tightening, the Fed has removed $2.0 trillion but still has a ways to go before getting back near the level of the portfolio before the Covid-19 pandemic hit.

As far as the policy rate of interest goes, the Fed has been working it down as it policy considerations dictate.

The Federal Reserve has a pathway that it has been following, and, so far, it has done a very good job, I believe, in staying on the path and bringing the Fed's balance sheet closer to where it needs to be.

But, I argue, the Fed still has a relatively long way to go.

Now, however, Fed officials have another problem.

The current U.S. president wants the Federal Reserve to follow another path. The path President Trump wants the Fed to follow is one of lower interest rates, with more financial stimulus to get the U.S. economy growing even faster.

Right now, if the Federal Reserve were to increase purchases of government securities to increase the size of the Fed's securities portfolio and reduce the Fed's policy rate of interest the major result would be a faster acceleration of the inflation rate.

My point is...there is already a substantial excess of "cash" in the banking system due to previous Federal Reserve action to combat the Covid-19 pandemic.

To do what Trump wants the Fed to do at this time would be just to pile excesses upon existing excesses.

And, I believe, that expectations in the commercial banking system are such that these expectations will explode.

The commercial banking system is already overloaded with excess cash!

To move to pump up the banking system further to drive down interest rates would only pump in more cash on top of the cash that is already there.

But, this seems to be the battle that is brewing...Trump versus Powell and the current Federal Reserve Board.

How this confrontation is resolved is going to have a lot to do with where interest rates, where the banking system, where the financial markets, where the price level, and where the economy goes.

The Trump monetary policy, one to keep real estate markets booming, is a policy for inflation. It always has been. It always will be.

Watch.

data.title: Markets That Shrug Off Bad News Want Higher (Technical Analysis)
data.description:
Market Analysis - Nasdaq (NASDAQ:QQQ)

I wrote last week that the market had been gappy and choppy, but that it was putting in a pattern of higher highs and higher lows, that it was starting to form the kind of flag pattern we'd ordinarily buy if it were a stock.

The gappy, volatile nature of the market was giving me fits - as a momentum trader, you want smoother moves after all - but I went back to the basics and looked carefully at the pattern of higher highs and higher lows. As long as those are in place, it tells you there is some sort of bid under the market.

This week, we got just what I was looking for as the market pushed up and out of this wedge pattern. I also noted a subtle change in character as the market was significantly less gappy. The only large gap, in fact, a -1% open on Wednesday in response to an above expectations CPI print, was bought immediately and aggressively by market makers all day.

Markets that shrug off bad news while trading up towards all-time highs are strong markets. And we had a decent amount of net negative news this week between the back and forth statements on tariffs from the President and internationally as well as the previously mentioned hot CPI report.

Markets that shrug off such news are markets that want higher. Bull markets climb a wall of worry.

Daily chart on the left, weekly on the right:

From a very short-term perspective, we are a little extended from moving averages with all-time highs on the left side acting as potential resistance (light gray line). It wouldn't surprise me to see the market chop around a little bit somewhere in the realm of those highs before pushing higher.

But, from an intermediate term perspective, this is a market I expect to trend higher, and I will position as such until it proves me wrong.

As I have noted repeatedly over the last weeks and months, I generally have a bullish bias right now, especially with an extremely pro business Trump Administration in place. Yes, there will be inevitable corrections. Yes, the odds do favor a risk event or panic at some point. But if this market tells me it wants to move higher, and we have the bullish political backdrop, I am going to do my best to ride that wave.

I watch the market closely to try to glean clues about the overall environment for taking new swing and momentum positions. Bad environments mean a lot of volatility or outright downward pressure and thus a higher likelihood of running stops on new positions where I do not yet have cushion. Good environments mean we have a trending market where new positions are more likely to immediately start working, allowing me to trail them for weeks or months against the moving averages.

Within that framework, this week’s action suggests to me, it's time to position more aggressively although with careful risk management and stops. As always, if the market fakes us out and turns back down, we do run the risk of hitting those stops, but on balance, at the start of a potential market trend, the greater risk is to be sitting on the sidelines.

Mid Cap Growth (IWP)

I follow the IWP because it correlates well with the smaller, growthier names I prefer to trade. These are the kinds of stocks that tend to have the most potential in a trending market, making bigger and faster moves than their large cap counterparts.

The IWP looks healthy. I noted last week that I saw some signs of short-term exhaustion in the realm of all-time highs. Sure enough, the IWP traded down to the 21 EMA (teal line) to start the week before reclaiming the 10 and 21 EMAs (pink and teal line), working off some of that extension and exhaustion.

To my eyes, this looks like it is ready to move higher.

Bitcoin (BTC)

While I am excited by what I see from the QQQ and IWP, to my eyes, Bitcoin seems fairly directionless while trading around in this larger range (light grey line overhead, light blue line underneath).

Long term, I have a bullish bias for Bitcoin. In the short and intermediate term, I need price to declare itself and tell me if it is more interested in breaking out to the upside or to the downside.

For now, I can't take anything actionable from this chart and will be setting Bitcoin, crypto, and its related stocks to the side although will keep it on my radar. I am not really going to do anything while it chops around in this box.

The next catalyst would be whatever Trump or his crypto czar decides to do.

It is worth noting that Trump's meme coin is down about 70% from highs:

As I have said before, it is an absolute, total, and complete disgrace that a President would pump and dump a meme coin onto his followers.

That said, I have trouble believing he is just going to set this to the side and watch it go to zero. It still has a 4.5 billion dollar market cap as of this writing. He has to know that if he gets the crypto market to get moving, his meme coin will inevitably do well too. I trade prices, but not psychology, but it is worth keeping in the back of mind that Trump has a multi-billion dollar reason to get crypto moving at some point.

Portfolio

I am currently sitting in more names than I would like and plan to cull the laggards in the coming week or two while adding to the winners if they give me good buy points.

Longs: Oklo (OKLO), CrowdStrike (CRWD), Tempus AI (TEM), Grail Inc. (GRAL), Sea Ltd. (SE), BigBear.ai (BBAI), Palantir (PLTR), Affirm (AFRM), Digital Turbine (APPS), Roblox (RBLX), Doximity (DOCS), Peloton (PTON), Shopify (SHOP), Innodata (INOD), DraftKings (DKNG), Archer Aviation (ACHR), Snowflake (SNOW).

Sadly, I sold Reddit (RDDT) after a bad earnings reaction after a 200%+ gain from my initial buy point. I do think we are not quite done with Reddit as a viable trading stock. My suspicion is that it will need a few weeks or months to consolidate after the crazy tear it went on in 2024 and to digest the earnings report, after which I will probably be revisiting it.

Story Stocks

In a hot market, you'll often find the companies that are on theme and sell a great story really moving. Yes, many of these are glorified science projects. Yes, many of them will be trading down 80% in the next year or two. No, that doesn't really matter to me if I can ride it to big gains. (For a longer discussion on this, please check out this article under the heading Destiny Tech100 (DXYZ), honestly this was probably one of the more important things I've written about momentum trading and bull markets.)

And the gains have been there if you are following themes that are moving and accumulation volume patterns. FYI - this is why I keep going back to the Quantum stocks to see if they have another leg in them.

OKLO is up about 90% from the buy point. I have discussed the nuclear theme and the AI power provider theme many times.

To my eyes, it looks like it wants to chop more or less sideways into earnings. My stops are trending on the 10 and 21 EMAs, and I will otherwise trim heavily into earnings in case there is a bad reaction.

BigBear.ai (BBAI) recently reported a DoD Contract to advance its Virtual Anticipation Network Prototype. It is up about 96% from the gap and go buy point on that news:

GRAL, maker of the incredibly exciting Galleri cancer detection product, is also up ~91% from the buy point:

Tempus AI (TEM), sitting at the intersection of AI and healthcare, and Digital Turbine (APPS), have also each done exceptionally well.

I'm not saying all this to brag about my winners. I've run stops in other names just like anyone else. Instead, I am saying this to point out the unbelievable power of focusing on the "in play" stocks in the hottest themes in a good market.

Watch for hot themes. Monitor for accumulation volume patterns. Find a tight consolidation area. Then buy on a breakout through that area. Simple but definitely not easy.

I am going to continue to watch for story stocks and theme stocks with big moves and big volume until the market tells me it won't work anymore.

Affirm (AFRM)

AFRM was probably the buy I was most excited about in the last week or two. I think the chart really speaks for itself.

In the weekly chart on the right, you can see that this thing completely fell apart after the 2020-2021 bubble and has been dead money for years, capped by that ~51.5 level (light gray line).

Recently, however, you'll note a marked change in character with the volume showing accumulation - high volume on up weeks, low volume on pullback weeks - and with the stock breaking out of that 51.5 level.

From there, it pulled back to 51.5 in an orderly fashion, putting in a nice base, before breaking out up and to multiyear highs on the back of a fantastic earnings report.

This looks like a stock that has just entered a "Stage 2 Uptrend" (from Stan Weinstein, a brilliant technician) and if its history is any guide, it has the potential to be a big winner.

Watch List

As mentioned, I am looking to cull some names and size up in some I already own more than I am looking to add a bunch of new names at present.

Balanced with that, though, is that we appear to be in a hot and story-driven market... so I am going to keep a close eye on where the volume and the buzz is going and be prepared to deploy there.

Aside from "story stocks" or "theme stocks," here is what I have been focusing on:

2020-2021 Winners that Developed Real Businesses

I think we're going to see some money rotate into some of the names that hit peaks in the 2020-2021 bubble and have been dead money since.

From a fundamental standpoint, these companies were dumped onto retail investors and then went up like rocket ships despite having no real earnings. The thing is... some of them have actually gone on to build real businesses, have real and growing revenue, and are starting to actually make money.

One of the most powerful patterns in technical analysis is when a "dead money" stock starts to wake up. I discussed this up above when I mentioned Stan Weinstein's Stage 2 Uptrend pattern.

We have already seen this with AFRM as noted above, but also with Roblox (RBLX) (long):

Roblox shows virtually the same pattern. Bubble highs. Dead money capped by a level, before showing accumulation volume and pushing through that level.

Sea Ltd (SE) shows the same pattern (also long):

Not all of these are going to reach their prior highs... but some will, and when they do, I intend to be there.

Upstart (UPST) is a fast-growing company that has a similar pattern and is high on my watch list.

Upstart has already started making some noise, and personally, I think it is for real this time. It just released a well regarded earnings report and has issued strong guidance. Look at the huge volume in that last week after a lower volume pullback.

Snowflake shows a similar pattern. I have a small long position but would like to get more aggressive if it gives me the opportunity and shows it wants to move higher.

Uber (UBER)

Some people love him. Some people hate him. But I am, for one, a big fan of Bill Ackman, who is a brilliant investor.

Uber has been a rocket ship this week on the news that Bill Ackman has taken a large stake in the company. This feels to me like the type of company that every growth oriented mutual fund is going to be stashing on their books over the coming years.

More importantly for me, the chart looks really good, especially with the backdrop of strong earnings and revenue growth. It has consolidated in a nearly years long base under that ~82 level and has started waking up over on that right side. I'd like to see it consolidate and set a tight supply/demand level somewhere near that 82 level and would plan to buy on a breakout through it.

Conclusion

As always, we will see what the market actually has in store for us. That said, with the market shrugging off bad news over the last month and breaking out of a flag pattern range near all-time highs, I have a bullish bias.

I am most focused on "story stocks" and "theme stocks" as well as 2020-2021 winners that have used the last 4 years to actually develop a business.

Remember, risk comes first. Trading is nothing more than a game of, on average and over the balance of many trades, selling stocks for more in gains than you stop out of in losses… then recycling that edge over again and again. If you want to learn more, the rabbit hole starts with William O'Neill's How to Make Money in Stocks, which may open your eyes to an entirely new investing philosophy and could give you the foundation you need to start momentum trading.

data.title: LKQ Corporation: They've Got Parts, They've Got Scale, They've Got My Attention
data.description:
Summary

LKQ Corporation (NASDAQ:LKQ) is a relatively larger player in the automotive parts and services industry, known for its wide range of product offerings and robust operational network. In this note, we’ll take a deep dive into LKQ's business operations, financial performance and competitive advantages. Additionally, we’ll address valuation and potential catalysts that could shape LKQ’s share price going forward. Overall, there is a lot to love with this company and I think this is a Buy opportunity.

The key points of my thesis include:

  • Undervalued from a historic basis

  • Strong historical financial performance and balance sheet

  • Promising growth prospects based on a size and scale advantage

Business Overview and Company Insights

LKQ is a leading distributor of replacement parts, components, and systems for repairs and maintenance. In addition to OEM recycled parts, they also offer aftermarket parts. LKQ distributes these products to collision and mechanical repair shops, coupled with after market collision and mechanical products, recycled collision and mechanical products, etc.. LKQ has operations in North America, Europe and Taiwan. More detail can be found in the 10-K.

There Are Many Factors To Consider, But The Key Consideration Is Consumer Behavior

Vehicle Sales

The obvious indicator for LKQ is vehicle sales, as greater turnover of purchased vehicles increases demand for maintenance and repair parts.

Consumer Confidence

Consumer confidence impacts discretionary spending, and car repairs can be deferred during economic downturns.

Used Car Values

Lower used car prices make vehicles more likely to be totaled rather than repaired after accidents

Repair Costs

Rising labor and parts costs can lead to more vehicles being totaled rather than repaired

Vehicle Miles Traveled (VMT) and Accident Rates

LKQ's business is closely tied to the number of vehicles on the road and accident rates. Metrics such as VMT, accident frequency, and severity could impact topline revenue. According to data from the National Highway Traffic Safety Administration (NHTSA), VMT has been increasing in recent years, which could be a tailwind for LKQ.

LKQ Possesses A Wide Range Of Parts Availability + Distribution Capabilities That Make It Hard To Beat

Scale and Distribution Network

LKQ has built an unmatched distribution infrastructure that allows for superior service levels, particularly evident after the Uni-Select acquisition. They can deliver parts multiple times per day to body shops, compared to competitors who might only deliver once or twice a week. This frequency and reliability create stronger customer relationships.

Product Breadth and Availability

LKQ offers a comprehensive solution by providing aftermarket parts, salvage parts, and now paint products through a single distribution channel. This "one-stop shop" approach is particularly valuable for body shops looking to streamline their supplier relationships. Their scale also allows them to maintain deeper inventory levels than competitors.

Cost Structure Advantages

Due to size and integrated network, LKQ can spread fixed costs across a larger revenue base. For example, when they added paint distribution through FinishMaster, they could leverage existing delivery routes and warehouse infrastructure, making their cost-to-serve significantly lower than standalone paint distributors.

Financial Strength

Despite a high-ish debt to equity ratio, LKQ still has the financial flexibility to invest in growth, weather downturns, and make strategic acquisitions when opportunities arise. Smaller competitors often lack this financial flexibility, particularly during challenging market conditions like the current environment. You may have noticed from other companies I’ve chosen to write about, that more or less, the companies I choose to cover usually possess this ability based on how I screen.

Employee Satisfaction

Traditional wisdom suggests higher employee satisfaction leads to better performance. 75% of employees say it's a great place to work—significantly above the 57% U.S. company average. In addition, 70% of LKQ employees would recommend their employer to a friend. Taking these two data points combined, it seems like the majority of employees have a favorable attitude towards the company.

Revenues Are Growing At A Nice Clip

LKQ has demonstrated consistent revenue growth, from roughly ~$6.7B in 2014 to ~$13.9B in 2023. However there was some volatility during this timeframe, such as a significant decline in 2020 to $11.6B due to COVID, followed by a strong recovery in 2021 with revenues of $13.1B. Quarterly revenues have also shown steady progression, with the most recent quarter showing a y/y increase. This sustained growth reflects LKQ's resilience and ability to expand its market share.

Margins Have Grown Since COVID, But Are They Starting To Dip?

With some notable fluctuations, LKQ's operating and profit margins have improved consistently and gradually. Since COVID, LKQ's operating margin peaked at 11.27% in 2022. However, we've seen the operating margin decline slightly in 2023 and stabilize so far into 2024. Despite these minor blips, LKQ has maintained steady profitability, proving their ability to thrive in tough economic times.

3Q24 Earnings Call Highlights

5 Key Takeaways:

  • Adjusted diluted EPS of $0.88, up 2% from prior year

  • $341M in FCF for the quarter

  • Repurchased 3M shares for approximately $125M

  • Board approved additional $1B share repurchase authorization through October 2026

  • Lowered full year 2024 guidance

LKQ's 3Q24 earnings present a mixed picture with both challenges and spots of brilliance. The primary concern is the significant decline in North American repairable claims, which dropped 9.5% year-over-year and is worse than Q2's decline. Management attributes this to rising insurance premiums and falling used car prices, which are causing consumers to delay or forego repairs. While they expect these headwinds to persist into early 2025, they anticipate improvement in 2H25 as used car prices stabilise. On the bright side, there are several positive indicators. Despite the revenue challenges, LKQ maintained strong cash flow generation ($341M FCF in 3Q24) and demonstrated commitment to shareholder returns through both dividends and share repurchases. The Board's approval of an additional $1B share repurchase authorization signals management's belief that the stock is undervalued.

Analyzing Debt Trends: How Leverage Impacts Company Performance

LKQ’s debt to equity is admittedly pushing the limit of what I’m comfortable with. LKQ's spike in debt to equity ratio in 2019 and again in 2023 are particularly noteworthy. These increases are from strategic decisions such as M&A. While a moderate amount of debt can fuel expansion, excessive reliance on it can elevate financial risk. Going forward, we will monitor whether LKQ will reduce its debt. I sense that the ratio will likely remain relatively stable in the short term, but it is important to reduce leverage to allow for financial flexibility in downturns.

Dividends Are Growing But The Track Record Is Short

LKQ has only been consistently paying dividends for a relatively short period, but has shown growth in its dividend payments thus far. LKQ's ability to maintain and potentially grow its dividend is supported by strong fundamental factors, including consistent free cash flow generation ($661M year-to-date as of 3Q24), an investment-grade credit rating, and stable market positions across multiple segments. While LKQ's dividend history may be shorter than some industrial companies, management has established a track record of reliable payments and shown commitment to maintaining them through various market conditions.

Share Counts Are Starting To Decline, Nice

LKQ has reduced its share count in the past decade, showing a shareholder-friendly approach. 292M shares were outstanding in 2010, but that went down to 268M in 2023. Over time, share count has steadily declined. As of 3Q24, shares outstanding further decreased to 263M. This consistent reduction in shares outstanding suggests LKQ's ongoing commitment to share buyback programs and improving per-share metrics

Continued Restructurings, M&A, FX And High Interest Rates Will Challenge The Expense Line

Restructuring + Transaction Expenses

LKQ experienced higher restructuring and transaction-related expenses at $99M YTD through 9/30/24, compared to $53M in 2023. The increase is due to LKQ's 2024 Global Restructuring Plan. $70M of the $99M is tied to this plan, which includes the sale of operations in Slovenia, Poland, and Bosnia. At best, LKQ's restructuring efforts signal a move towards improved profitability.

Interest Expenses

LKQ also saw a significant rise in interest expenses, hitting $196M YTD through 9/30/24, up from $150M in 2023. This jump in interest expense is largely because of higher debt tied to the Uni-Select acquisition. The effect of rising interest expenses on LKQ's profitability warrants consideration. Given we are now in a rate cutting cycle, I don't see this type of expense to move significantly higher. However, rate is only one part of the equation. Investors should also pay attention to management's ability to manage its capital and funding needs.

FX Introducing Volatility Into The P&L

LKQ recorded a $49M gain fromFX contracts related to acquisitions YTD through 9/30/24.. The unpredictability of these FX impacts on the P&L make it difficult to account for in an earnings model, but we’ll try our best to take any hints from management where we can. Oddly enough, the foreign exchange gain almost completely offsets the elevated interest expense from the same transaction.

The Valuation Points Upward

My blended valuation of LKQ is ~$70 / share based on a 1-year forward look of earnings and free cash flow, coupled with the recent addition of using an industry avg PE. The line of thinking with the latter is that my belief is this company is performing better than average, so it should at least trade at a multiple that's higher than the industry average. Compared to currently traded levels at around $38 a share, there’s some significant upside here. The NTM earnings figure should bump up my estimate after I switch to adjusted EPS, as opposed to reported. But luckily, I have FCF as a fallback since these estimates aren’t precise.

Higher Than Street On Revenue, Tougher On Earnings

My FY24 top line estimates are more optimistic than Street, but my EPS estimates are significantly below. This will need to be fixed in the future, as I believe the Street is reporting an adjusted EPS figure, while I’m using the reported figure. Even if I discounted the Street’s figures, I’m still coming in on the low side, so my expense control estimates aren’t as optimistic. Overall, I believe that sales will still hold, particularly in the US as the economy is still very healthy with low unemployment figures that should continue to drive consumer spending in the next year or two.

A Recession Will Be The Key Headwind, Apart From That There IS Room to Run Higher

Auto Parts Industry Consolidation

LKQ has a history of successful acquisitions, and further consolidation could lead to more market share. LKQ can boost its position within its space by acquiring and integrating smaller players.

Used Vehicle Market

As the used car market continues to expand, driven by factors such as rising new car prices and changing consumer preferences, demand for aftermarket parts is likely to increase.

EVs Entering The Mix

LKQ is adapting to new tech and market trends, which was once a threat but is now a tailwind. LKQ can take advantage of the growing electric vehicle market by developing and distributing EV-specific replacement parts.

The Obvious Recession Risk

A recession could pose a significant risk to LKQ's business, as it would for pretty much any other business out there. The line of thinking is that consumers may be forced to cut back on discretionary spending, including vehicle repairs and maintenance. In challenging times, consumers may be forced to delay or forego non-essential repairs, leading to decreased demand for aftermarket parts and services. A counterargument to this is that individuals may opt to repair in place of purchasing a replacement vehicle.

Conclusion

Wrapping things up, I think LKQ is a solid business with strong fundamentals, a wide distribution network, and a track record of steady revenue growth. Management’s ability to navigate industry challenges is impressive. That said, the valuation offers enough margin of safety for me to be fully on board at current levels.
